front: Poem: opens 'Between my finger and my thumb / The squat pen rests; snug as a gun' and ends with 'I'll dig with it'
Poem: the speaker delivers his grandfather 'milk in a bottle / Corked sloppily with paper' while the old man cuts turf on Toner's bog
Poem: ends 'A four-foot box, a foot for every year' describing a dead brother's coffin
Poem: the dead brother wears 'a poppy bruise on his left temple' after 'the bumper knocked him clear'
Poem: opens 'I sat all morning in the college sick bay'; Big Jim Evans says 'it was a hard blow'
Poem: the speaker's 'palms sticky as Bluebeard's' while picking the title fruit
Poem: 'a rat-grey fungus' spoils the harvest; ends 'Each year I hoped they'd keep, knew they would not'
Poem: frogs described as 'great slime kings' who are 'gathered there for vengeance' at a flax-dam
Poem: the speaker fills 'jampotfuls of the jellied specks' of frogspawn; frogs are 'poised like mud grenades'
Poem: the speaker wants to visit Aarhus to see a 'peat-brown head'; ends 'In the old man-killing parishes / I will feel lost, unhappy and at home'
work: The Tollund Man and Bog Body Poems

front: Poem: a figure 'seems to weep the black river of himself'; the speaker asks 'Who will say corpse to his vivid cast?'
work: The Tollund Man and Bog Body Poems

front: Poem: begins 'Here is the girl's head' — about a decapitated bog body, sharing its title with an Abel Meeropol poem
work: The Tollund Man and Bog Body Poems

front: Author: this poet's verse translation of Beowulf opens with 'So.' in place of 'Hwæt!' and uses a style called 'Scullionspeak'
Poem: the speaker's childhood hobby of peering into wells is now 'beneath all adult dignity'; ends 'I rhyme / To see myself, to set the darkness echoing'
Poem: the father's shoulders are 'globed like a full sail' and he leaves a 'hobnailed wake'; ends with the father 'stumbling / Behind me, and will not go away'
Poem: describes a conversation with 'an English journalist in search of views / On the Irish thing'; asks 'Is there a life before death?'
Author: adapted Sophocles' Philoctetes as The Cure at Troy, containing the passage about 'the longed-for tidal wave of justice'; read at Biden's inauguration
Author: founding member of the Field Day Theatre Company with Brian Friel and Seamus Deane
Author: declared 'No lyric has ever stopped a tank' in the essay 'The Government of the Tongue'
Author: translated the medieval Irish tale Buile Suibhne as Sweeney Astray; the character Sweeney appears in the collection Station Island
Poem: describes 'Centuries / Of fear and homage to the famine god' and 'stinking potatoes' that 'fouled the land'
Poem: concludes 'on well-run farms pests have to be kept down'; about the speaker seeing a man drown kittens in a bucket
Poem: a man 'sure-footed but too sly' was 'blown to bits / out drinking in a curfew'
Poem: dedicated to the poet's mother; describes a felled chestnut tree as 'a soul ramifying and forever / silent, beyond silence listened for'
